summary:
The Paris Commune was a radical socialist government that ruled the French capital for 72 days from 18 March to 28 May 1871, in the chaotic aftermath of the [[event:2535|Franco-Prussian War]]. Born from the conjunction of military defeat, national humiliation, and deep social tensions, it became the most significant working-class insurrection of the nineteenth century and a defining reference point for socialist and revolutionary thought.

The Commune arose when the French National Assembly, dominated by conservatives, attempted to disarm the Paris National Guard and collect debts that had been suspended during the German siege of the city. Parisian workers and National Guardsmen refused to surrender their cannon; two generals were seized and shot, and the government fled to Versailles. The Commune took power and held elections, resulting in a governing council dominated by socialists, anarchists, and radical republicans.

The Communard government implemented a series of radical measures during its brief existence: it remitted rents due during the siege, abolished night work in bakeries, handed closed workshops over to worker cooperatives, separated church and state, and decreed free secular education. It was the first government in history to be substantially composed of and directed by working-class people, and its measures anticipated reforms that would take decades to achieve in more conservative societies.

The French government at Versailles, having concluded an armistice with Germany, used its liberated regular army to reconquer Paris in May 1871 during 'Bloody Week' (La Semaine Sanglante). The fighting was savage; the Communards executed hostages including the Archbishop of Paris, and in their last days set fire to public buildings. The Versailles troops killed somewhere between 10,000 and 30,000 Communards and Parisian civilians, and imprisoned or deported tens of thousands more. Marx analyzed the Commune in The Civil War in France as the first example of the 'dictatorship of the proletariat,' and it became a sacred founding myth of the socialist and communist movements.
